macro_summary: Markets enter the week in recovery mode after the June 17 FOMC decision — the Fed's first under Chair Kevin Warsh — held rates at 3.50–3.75% but delivered a hawkish shock: nine of 18 officials penciled in at least one rate hike in 2026, a sharp reversal from March's two-cut projection, sending short-term yields higher and equities lower mid-week. A second major macro shift is the US-Iran peace MOU signed June 18, which has reopened the Strait of Hormuz and pushed crude oil roughly 5% lower from its recent highs, easing inflation pressures but still leaving WTI ~40% above its year-start level. Today's session is light on scheduled data, but May PCE and final Q1 GDP third estimate are due June 25 — four days out — and will be closely watched for clues on whether the Fed's hawkish tilt is warranted. The dominant sector story is semiconductors: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index has surged 60–80% YTD on AI capex momentum, and Micron reports earnings today after the close, making it the single highest-conviction event on the calendar.
key_events: FOMC (June 17): Fed held at 3.50–3.75%, but dot plot shifted hawkish — 9 of 18 officials now project ≥1 rate hike in 2026; CPI running at 4.2% YoY in May
US-Iran MOU signed June 18: Strait of Hormuz reopening; WTI crude fell ~5% to ~$80/bbl on June 15 but still up ~40% YTD; full energy-supply normalization expected to take months

Intel/Apple chip deal (June 18): Trump announced via Truth Social that Apple will work with Intel to design/manufacture chips domestically; Intel surged ~10%; deal formally unconfirmed by either company
Micron (MU) Q3 FY2026 earnings: due tonight after market close (June 21); consensus ~$19.95 EPS on ~$34.66B revenue; company guidance was $33.5B ± $0.75B with ~81% gross margin
June 25: May PCE price index + GDP final Q1 third estimate due — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, critical given hawkish dot-plot shift
SpaceX (SPCX) listed on Nasdaq June 13; options began trading this week
NVDA S&P 500 index inclusion effective June 22 (Monday)

ideas.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
ideas.thesis: Micron reports Q3 FY2026 earnings tonight after the close — the most concrete, dated catalyst in today's session. The company has guided for record revenue of $33.5B ± $0.75B and ~81% gross margins, and has beaten EPS consensus by 33–42% in each of the last two quarters as HBM memory demand from AI hyperscalers has proven consistently stronger than Street estimates. Wall Street has been aggressively revising targets upward, with RBC raising its target to $1,200 on June 15 alone, and Cantor Fitzgerald's most aggressive target sitting at $1,500 — versus today's price near $1,134.
ideas.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings release tonight, June 21 after market close (confirmed); Q4 guidance commentary on HBM allocation, DRAM pricing, and CapEx trajectory will be the incremental mover
ideas.edge: Consensus EPS of $19.95 vs company-guided midpoint of $19.15 implies Street is already modeling an above-guidance beat. RBC target $1,200 and Cantor target $1,500 vs current ~$1,134 imply 6–32% upside from the most recently updated analysts; Q2 saw actual EPS of $12.20 vs Street estimate of $8.60 — a 42% beat — establishing a pattern of material upside surprise
ideas.bull_case: HBM capacity sold out through 2026 creates pricing power; if gross margin guidance is raised above 81% for Q4 and full-year CapEx commentary remains disciplined, the stock could re-rate toward the $1,200–$1,500 analyst target range. AI infrastructure spending shows no signs of slowing, with every major cloud provider expanding data center capex.
ideas.bear_case: The stock has surged 297% YTD and trades at a $1.28T market cap — GF Value estimates it at 191% overvalued at current prices. Any softness in Q4 guidance (e.g., hyperscaler order delays, DRAM pricing moderation, or tariff/geopolitical language) could trigger a sharp post-earnings selloff. Insiders sold $92.5M in shares over the last three months.

ideas.name: NVIDIA Corporation
ideas.thesis: Nvidia's S&P 500 index inclusion becomes effective at the open Monday June 22, creating near-term forced buying from passive index funds. Separately, the stock trades at a forward P/E of just 25.4x despite posting record fiscal 2026 revenue of $215.9B — a stark valuation discount to peers like AMD (84.4x forward P/E) that have rallied further on the same AI capex theme. The US-Iran peace deal also incrementally improves the macro environment for rate-sensitive high-multiple tech, as oil-driven inflation pressure eases.
ideas.catalyst: S&P 500 index inclusion effective June 22 (Monday) driving passive index-fund buying; Micron earnings tonight may also act as a proxy read-through on AI infrastructure demand that benefits NVDA sentiment
ideas.edge: Forward P/E of 25.4x vs AMD's 84.4x on comparable AI infrastructure exposure — Nvidia trades at a roughly 70% valuation discount to its nearest competitor despite higher revenue scale ($215.9B) and faster growth (65% YoY revenue); passive index inclusion flows are a mechanical near-term demand catalyst independent of fundamentals
ideas.bull_case: Index inclusion creates forced buying regardless of macro conditions; if Micron tonight signals continued AI hyperscaler capex strength, NVDA re-rates as the primary GPU beneficiary. The valuation gap to AMD gives room for multiple expansion.
ideas.bear_case: SOXX has already surged 45–50% since March and RSI was recently above 80 — deeply overbought. If Micron disappoints tonight or Q4 guidance is weak, the entire AI chip complex could sell off in sympathy regardless of NVDA's own fundamentals. The hawkish Fed dot plot also compresses high-multiple tech valuations if rate hike odds increase.

ideas.name: U.S. Global Jets ETF
ideas.thesis: The US-Iran MOU signed June 18 and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz represent a structural fuel cost inflection for the airline industry, which had been absorbing a 121% YoY surge in jet fuel prices and an IATA-estimated $100B increase in collective fuel costs for 2026. The IEA characterized the Hormuz closure as 'the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.' Airlines were among the first movers on June 15 (up 4–9%), but the full margin benefit is a multi-quarter story as hedges roll off and lower spot fuel prices flow through income statements.
ideas.catalyst: Strait of Hormuz physically reopening in the coming weeks as mine clearance and insurance normalization proceed; Q2 earnings season for airlines (mid-July) will be the first opportunity to quantify the fuel cost tailwind in guidance
ideas.edge: IATA pre-deal projected 2026 industry net profits at just $23B vs $45B in 2025 — the deal, if sustained, substantially closes that gap. Jet fuel prices had surged 121% YoY by April 2026 vs. ~$90/barrel baseline in 2025; WTI is now ~$80–81/barrel and trending lower. JETS ETF provides diversified exposure across UAL, DAL, AAL, LUV, ALK, and others without single-carrier execution risk.
ideas.bull_case: If oil continues to normalize toward $70–75 as Hormuz throughput recovers over the next 2–3 months, airline fuel costs could fall 20–30% from peak, dramatically improving 2H 2026 earnings. Forward bookings typically lag fare adjustments, meaning revenue holds even as costs decline.
ideas.bear_case: The initial rally (4–9% on June 15) may have already priced in most of the deal optimism; full Hormuz normalization could take many months per the US Energy Secretary, and oil inventories are at their lowest since 1983 — meaning prices may not fall as far as markets expect. A hawkish Fed that follows through on a rate hike would also compress consumer spending and travel demand.
